1.exposition: The verdict of chapter 2 has seemed to strip Israel of every distinction, and Paul now lets the wounded Jewish conscience speak: “What advantage then has the Jew?” He answers without hesitation — “Much in every way” — chiefly that Israel was entrusted with the very oracles of God. Then a harder objection presses: if some Israelites proved unfaithful, has their failure cancelled God’s faithfulness? “By no means!” — “Let God be true though every one were a liar” (citing Psalms 51:4). Paul then fends off the slander that his gospel amounts to “let us do evil that good may come,” pronouncing its condemnation just. The privilege of Israel is real, and God’s covenant fidelity unshaken — yet neither exempts the Jew from the sentence about to fall on all.
1.original_language: The privilege Paul names is possession of the logia tou theou (“oracles of God”, v.2) — the divine utterances, Israel’s Scriptures with their law, prophets, and Messianic promises. The objection of v.3 turns on a deliberate wordplay: some ēpistēsan (“were unfaithful / did not believe” — apisteō holds both senses), yet this cannot nullify tēn pistin tou theou, “the faithfulness of God” (an objective genitive: not faith directed at God but God’s own fidelity). Twice the passage rings with Paul’s emphatic mē genoito (“may it never be, God forbid”, vv.4, 6) — the recoil of the diatribe, not a mild denial.
1.historical_context: Paul resumes the diatribē, the lecture-hall style of question and emphatic rebuttal, voicing an imagined Jewish interlocutor more sharply than any real opponent would and then answering him. In an oral culture where written Scripture was rare and costly, to be entrusted with God’s own words was an extraordinary honor (cf. Psalms 147:19–20). His defense of God’s truthfulness draws on David’s confession in Psalms 51:4, where the sinner’s guilt only vindicates the Judge. The charge that Paul taught “do evil that good may come” was a genuine distortion of his grace-gospel that he had to rebut repeatedly (cf. Romans 6:1).
1.christ: Paul’s defense of God’s truthfulness and justice (vv.4–6) quietly raises the very question the gospel will answer: how can God be at once “just and the justifier”? That tension is resolved only at the cross. The oracles entrusted to Israel (v.2) themselves testify to the righteousness now revealed in Christ (v.21), and God’s covenant faithfulness, which no human unbelief could overturn (v.3), finds its Yes in the Son through whom every promise of God is confirmed (cf. 2 Corinthians 1:20).

verses.note: “What advantage then has the Jew?” Having shown the Jew, like the Gentile, under sin and circumcision profitless without obedience, Paul anticipates the natural recoil. grammatical-historical Robertson hears the practiced diatribē — the apostle poses the objector’s question in order to answer it. reformed Calvin feels the tension: having seemingly stripped Israel of all dignity, Paul asks whether any advantage remains — and will grant that it does, but as God’s free gift, not the Jew’s merit.

“Much in every way; chiefly the oracles of God.” grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson note that logia means the divine utterances — the Old Testament Scriptures, the law and prophets and promises of Messiah — committed to Israel as depositary and dispenser of God’s word; eastern Theodoret sees in this the supreme honor of the nation. reformed Calvin fixes the value in the word itself, not the bare sign, given by free favor — so that carelessness with such a trust makes ingratitude the worse.

“For what if some were unfaithful?” grammatical-historical Robertson and eastern Theodoret mark the softening — some, not all — and the double sense of apisteō (unbelief and unfaithfulness together). Does such failure void “the faithfulness of God”? reformed Calvin gives the answer already implied: God’s truth is firm and constant, not destroyed by man’s perfidy; though most broke the covenant, it kept its efficacy for the believing remnant.

“Let God be true though every one were a liar.” grammatical-historical Robertson catches the vehemence of mē genoito; grammatical-historical Gill and Barnes hear the great principle — God’s veracity stands though every man fail (echoing Psalms 116:11). Paul clinches it with David’s confession from Psalms 51:4: God is “justified in his words” and overcomes when judged — even human sin serving, against its will, to magnify his righteousness.

Now the perverse inference: if our unrighteousness shows God’s righteousness, is God then unjust to inflict wrath? grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson expose it as a sophistry that would abuse God’s glory to escape judgment. Paul’s parenthesis — “I speak in a human way” — marks the cavil as a carnal manner of reasoning, not his own; reformed Calvin names its danger, the making of God’s glory into an excuse for sin, which he rejects as blasphemous.

“By no means! For then how could God judge the world?” grammatical-historical Robertson notes the emphatic recoil; grammatical-historical Barnes and reformed Calvin draw out the reductio: were God unjust to punish the sin that magnifies him, he could not judge the world at all — yet that God will judge the world is axiomatic even to the objector. The cavil destroys itself.

The same objection sharpened into the first person: if my falsehood magnifies God’s truth, why am I still condemned as a sinner? grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson note the plausible ring of it — sin made a benefit, condemnation made unjust — and that Paul restates the reasoning precisely in order to press it toward its absurd and blasphemous conclusion in the next verse.

There the reasoning lands: “Let us do evil that good may come” — the antinomian distortion of his gospel that opponents, grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson observe, slanderously reported as his own teaching. Paul does not argue further but pronounces sentence: “their condemnation is just.” reformed Calvin reads the curt severity as the measure of his abhorrence — grace is never a license to sin.

witnesses.point: Israel’s advantage is real but wholly of God’s free favor, not merit; and God’s truth stands firm though every man prove a liar, the covenant keeping its efficacy for the believing remnant.
Paul works the diatribe — posing the objector’s questions to answer them — and recoils from each perverse inference with his emphatic mē genoito.
The chief advantage is the logia, God’s own oracles entrusted to Israel; the cavil that sin magnifies God and so should escape wrath is a sophistry Paul exposes and dismisses.

external.name: N.T. Wright
external.point: Reads the whole exchange as a defense of God’s covenant faithfulness (his dikaiosynē as fidelity to his promises): the questions of Israel’s privilege and God’s reliability raised here are not settled now but held over for the sustained argument of chapters 9–11.

1.application: Assurance that rests on our own steadiness will always waver; assurance that rests on “God be true though every one were a liar” cannot. And note the guardrail Paul sets: the God who brings good out of our evil never thereby approves the evil. Grace magnified is never grace to sin.

9.exposition: Paul now draws the long indictment to its verdict. Are we Jews any better? “No, not at all — for we have already charged that all, both Jews and Greeks, are under sin.” Then, that the sentence may fall with the weight of God’s own word, he lets Scripture pronounce it, stringing a chain of quotations that opens with “none is righteous, no, not one” and moves down the body — throat, tongue, lips, mouth, feet, eyes (vv.13–18) — until every faculty stands accused. Whatever the law says it says to those under it, “so that every mouth may be stopped, and the whole world held accountable to God.” The conclusion is decisive: “by works of the law no human being will be justified, since through the law comes the knowledge of sin.” Every defense is silenced, and the ground is cleared for grace.
9.original_language: To be hyph’ hamartian (“under sin”, v.9) is to be under sin as a ruling power, a dominion holding all captive — not merely a tally of misdeeds. The verb Paul uses for his prior indictment, proaitiaomai (“to charge beforehand”), is a courtroom word: the case has already been made. The catena itself is a testimonia chain, and its verdict is exceptionless — the twice-sounded oude heis (“not even one”). The summary of v.20 turns on ou … pasa sarx (“not … all flesh,” a Hebraism for “no one”, echoing Psalms 143:2) and on the law’s true office, epignōsis hamartias, the full “knowledge of sin.”
9.historical_context: The chain of citations (vv.10–18) is woven from Psalms 14:1–3, Psalms 5:9, Psalms 140:3, Psalms 10:7, Isaiah 59:7–8, and Psalms 36:1 — the rabbinic “string of pearls” (ḥaruz) technique, threading texts from across the canon to show that universal condemnation is the settled testimony of Israel’s own Scriptures. “Every mouth stopped” is the forensic image of a defendant with no answer left, fallen silent before the judge; and “works of the law” (v.20) echoes Psalms 143:2, “no one living is righteous before you.”
9.christ: The universal verdict is the dark foil the gospel needs. A race enslaved “under sin” (v.9) and “not seeking for God” cannot save itself, and the law only deepens the diagnosis it cannot cure. By proving that no one is justified “by works of the law” (v.20), Paul clears the one space in which the cross can be received as sheer gift. The single truly Righteous One stands outside this indictment — and it is precisely the sinless Christ who will be “made sin” for the unrighteous (cf. 2 Corinthians 5:21) to supply the righteousness none of them possess.

verses.note: “Are we any better? No, not at all.” grammatical-historical Robertson presses the verb proaitiaomai — Paul has already charged beforehand both Jews (chapter 2) and Gentiles (chapter 1) with being “under sin.” eastern Chrysostom and Theodoret hear the great leveling: all alike stand under sin’s guilt, power, and condemnation, justly arraigned before the one tribunal.

The catena opens: “None is righteous, no, not one” (Psalms 14:1–3). grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson note the method — Paul proves universal sin from the Jews’ own revered Scriptures, so the conclusion cannot be evaded; reformed Calvin marks the exceptionless force, that all are destitute of righteousness before God.

“No one understands; no one seeks for God” (Psalms 14:2). grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson read a two-fold ruin — the mind darkened so that none discerns divine things, the will averse so that none, by nature, seeks God. reformed Calvin adds that man does not merely fail to find God but flees from him, and so must be both enlightened and drawn.

“All have turned aside; together they have become worthless” (Psalms 14:3). grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson trace the apostasy — all wandered from the way, all become unprofitable; eastern Cyril and Theodoret dwell on the emphatic exclusion of any exception, “no, not one,” the corruption reaching the whole race together.

Now the organs of speech: “Their throat is an open grave” (Psalms 5:9; Psalms 140:3). grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson savor the vivid imagery — the throat like an open sepulchre exhaling death, the tongue practiced in deceit; eastern Theodoret dwells on the asp’s venom hidden under the lips, slander that kills.

“Their mouth is full of curses and bitterness” (Psalms 10:7). grammatical-historical Barnes and Ellicott note the overflow of a corrupt heart — imprecation hurled against God and men, and the settled rancor that goes with it — the fountain betrayed by what pours from the mouth.

From speech the catena turns to deeds: “Their feet are swift to shed blood” (Isaiah 59:7; Proverbs 1:16). grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson mark the eagerness — not reluctant but hastening to violence, running toward the harm that fallen nature is bent upon.

“In their paths are ruin and misery” (Isaiah 59:7). grammatical-historical Barnes and reformed Calvin note the wreckage sin leaves behind it — destruction to others and wretchedness in its wake — sin producing not blessing but ruin wherever it is suffered to reign.

“And the way of peace they have not known” (Isaiah 59:8). grammatical-historical Barnes and reformed Calvin note the deprivation: those given to violence and ruin are strangers to true peace — neither peace with God nor peace among men — the opposite of the way they walk.

The catena climaxes: “There is no fear of God before their eyes” (Psalms 36:1). grammatical-historical Barnes, Robertson, and JFB, with reformed Calvin, name this the root and fountain from which every corruption of mind, speech, and deed has flowed. puritan Matthew Henry seals it: where no fear of God is, no good is to be looked for.

“Whatever the law says, it says to those under the law” — grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson note the point closes the Jew’s escape, for the quoted Scriptures are his own and speak to him. The law’s aim is that “every mouth may be stopped” and eastern Chrysostom and puritan Matthew Henry mark the reach — the whole world, Jew and Gentile, brought silent and guilty before God.

The great negative conclusion: “by works of the law no flesh will be justified, for through the law comes the knowledge of sin.” grammatical-historical Barnes, Robertson, and Clarke read the law’s office as diagnostic — a mirror that exposes crookedness but cannot straighten it. On “works of the law,” reformed Calvin argues forcefully (against Chrysostom and Origen) that Paul means the whole law, moral included, since the sins charged were moral — no deeds of any law can justify.

witnesses.point: The verdict levels all: Jew and Gentile alike stand under sin’s guilt and power, arraigned before one bar, so that every mouth is stopped and the whole world made guilty before God.
Paul has already 'charged beforehand' (proaitiaomai) both groups; the catena of Scriptures then proves universal sin from the Jews' own canon, verdict falling on the whole person.
The corruption reaches mind and will alike, and 'works of the law' means the whole law, moral included — for the sins charged are moral, and no law-keeping can justify a sinner before God.
'No fear of God' is the root of all the rest; where the fear of God is wanting, no good can be looked for, and the law's work is to silence every plea.

external.name: James D.G. Dunn
external.point: Argues that 'works of the law' (v.20) refers first to the identity-markers — circumcision, food laws, Sabbath — by which Israel fenced its covenant status, so Paul is denying that any such badge secures acquittal; the older reading (Calvin, Moo) takes the phrase more broadly of all deeds done in obedience to law, and the two readings frame a still-live debate.

9.application: The passage will not let self-righteousness survive. Before the diagnosis of vv.10–18 no one comes to God as a creditor. But note what the silencing is for: only when every mouth is stopped (v.19) is the heart finally ready to receive a righteousness it could never earn.

21.exposition: Then comes the great turn on which the whole letter pivots: “But now the righteousness of God has been manifested apart from the law” — yet not without witness, for the Law and the Prophets attested it beforehand. It is a righteousness “through faith in Jesus Christ for all who believe,” and there is no distinction, for all alike have sinned and fall short of the glory of God. Sinners with no righteousness of their own are “justified by his grace as a gift, through the redemption that is in Christ Jesus,” whom God “set forth as a propitiation by his blood, to be received by faith” — so demonstrating his righteousness in having passed over former sins, that he might be “just and the justifier of the one who has faith in Jesus.” Therefore boasting is “excluded,” the one God justifies Jew and Gentile on the same terms of faith, and faith does not overthrow but upholds the law. This is the theological heart of Romans, and of the gospel itself.
21.original_language: The hinge is nyni de (“but now”, v.21), the eschatological turn: a new moment has arrived in Christ. To dikaioō (“justify”) is a forensic verb — to declare righteous in court, not to make inwardly good; apolytrōsis (“redemption”, v.24) is release by ransom, the language of manumission and of the Exodus (Exodus 6:6), given dōrean (“as a free gift”). The pivotal word is hilastērion (v.25): the Septuagint’s term for the “mercy seat,” the blood-sprinkled lid of the ark (Leviticus 16:14–15) — hence “propitiation” (the removal of wrath) or, some urge, “expiation” (the wiping-away of sin); the term carries both the covering of sin and the turning-away of judgment. A second debated genitive is pistis Iēsou Christou (v.22): traditionally an objective genitive, “faith in Christ” (so the internal witnesses), though some recent interpreters (N.T. Wright and others) render the subjective “faithfulness of Christ.” Finally paresis (v.25, the “passing over” of past sins) is deliberately distinct from full aphesis, remission.
21.historical_context: “Redemption” evokes the slave market and the first Exodus — deliverance from bondage secured by a price God himself pays. “Propitiation” reaches back to the Day of Atonement, when the high priest sprinkled blood on the mercy seat behind the veil (Leviticus 16:14–15); but where that rite was hidden in the Holy of Holies, Christ was “set forth” — displayed publicly, in the open, upon the cross. The “glory of God” that all fall short of is the dignity humanity was created to bear. And the “former sins passed over” are the sins of the ages before Christ, which God in forbearance had not fully requited — a forbearance the cross now vindicates as righteous.
21.christ: Here the cross is interpreted with unmatched depth. Christ is the “propitiation by his blood” — the true mercy seat where sin is covered and wrath turned away, the place of atonement set forth in the open. His death is “redemption,” the ransom that frees the enslaved. Most profoundly, the cross shows God to be “just and the justifier of the one who has faith in Jesus”: he neither overlooks sin nor condemns the believing sinner, because his own Son bears the judgment in their place (cf. 2 Corinthians 5:21). In Christ, mercy and justice meet; the righteousness of God is at once vindicated and freely given away.

verses.note: “But now the righteousness of God has been manifested apart from law.” grammatical-historical Robertson and Ellicott mark the emphatic nyni de — having proved all under sin, Paul turns to God’s way of justifying. reformed Calvin reads “the righteousness of God” as his plan of making men righteous, not by their obedience but by another way; yet eastern Theodoret and puritan Matthew Henry stress that it is no novelty — the law and the prophets foretold and attested it.

“The righteousness of God through faith in Jesus Christ for all who believe.” grammatical-historical Robertson and Barnes, with reformed Calvin, take “faith of Jesus Christ” as an objective genitive — faith directed to Christ, its object, not Christ’s own faith, and faith as instrument, never merit. (Some moderns revive the subjective sense, “the faithfulness of Christ,” but the witnesses read it of the believer’s faith.) eastern Theodoret notes the reach — “unto all and upon all” — there is no difference.

“For all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God.” grammatical-historical Robertson and Gill note the aorist hēmarton gathering the whole race into one act and fact of sin; the present husterountai marks the continuing shortfall. Robertson, Barnes, and Calvin take “the glory of God” as his approbation (cf. John 12:43) — all fall short of the praise that comes from God. This universal ground is why there is “no difference.”

“Justified freely by his grace through the redemption in Christ Jesus.” grammatical-historical Barnes, Robertson, and wesleyan-arminian Wesley press dōrean — a free, wholly undeserved gift — and apolytrōsis, a releasing by ransom, the price paid to free captives. reformed Calvin lays out the causes: God’s mercy the efficient, Christ’s blood the meritorious, faith the instrumental, God’s glory the final; eastern Theodoret, that Christ offered his own body as the ransom price.

The atonement stated fully: God “set him forth as a propitiation … by his blood.” On hilastērion the witnesses divide: grammatical-historical Barnes and Clarke, with eastern Theodoret, take it as the mercy seat — Christ the true place where God is reconciled; grammatical-historical Robertson and reformed Calvin lean to “propitiatory sacrifice,” that which renders God propitious. Both hold the substance: Christ the means of atonement, his blood the ground, faith the instrument. The “passing over of former sins” (paresis, not full remission) shows God righteous even in his long forbearance.

The crux, and the crown of the gospel: that God might be “just and the justifier of the one who has faith in Jesus.” grammatical-historical Robertson — “nowhere has Paul put the problem of God more acutely” — with Barnes and JFB, marks the whole difficulty and its solution: God upholds the integrity of his law and character, refusing to pardon without atonement, and yet acquits the believing sinner. To forgive without the cross would sacrifice justice; the cross alone reconciles justice and mercy.

“Then what becomes of boasting? It is excluded.” grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson (“shut out”) note that justification by grace through faith bars all self-glory — not by a “law of works,” which might leave room to boast, but by the “law of faith,” which receives everything and brings nothing of its own. puritan Matthew Henry and reformed Calvin: faith ascribes the whole of salvation to God’s mercy.

“A man is justified by faith apart from works of the law.” grammatical-historical Barnes calls this the grand peculiarity of the gospel — Luther’s “article on which the church stands or falls” — with Robertson noting the summary verb, Paul’s settled reckoning. reformed Calvin harmonizes James: James asks how faith is proved genuine (justified before men), Paul how the sinner is accounted righteous before God — different questions, one truth. Works are the fruit, never the ground.

“Is God the God of Jews only? Is he not the God of Gentiles also?” grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson draw the inference: since justification is by faith and not by Jewish privilege, it is open to all — therefore God is God of all. reformed Calvin sees God’s design to make himself known as Savior to the nations, opening the kingdom of Christ to the whole world.

“God is one, who will justify the circumcised by faith and the uncircumcised through faith.” grammatical-historical Barnes, Ellicott, and Robertson ground the unity of salvation in the unity of God: one God, therefore one way for all. The slight variation — “by faith … through faith” — means essentially the same; reformed Calvin hears gentle irony: if any insists on a difference between Jew and Gentile, let him take that one, for it is the same faith either way.

“Do we then overthrow the law by this faith? By no means; we uphold the law.” grammatical-historical Barnes and Ellicott meet the antinomian objection; the positive answer, they and Clarke and reformed Calvin note, is manifold — God honored the law in requiring atonement rather than pardoning without it, Christ fulfilled and magnified it, and the justified believer, faith working through love, is led to obey it. grammatical-historical Clarke warns plainly: faith that does not work by love is not the faith that justifies.

witnesses.point: 'Nowhere has Paul put the problem of God more acutely' than in 'just and the justifier' (v.26): God upholds his law's integrity, refusing to pardon without atonement, yet acquits the believer — the cross reconciling justice and mercy.

Lays out the causes of justification — God's mercy efficient, Christ's blood meritorious, faith instrumental, God's glory final — and harmonizes James: Paul speaks of being accounted righteous before God, James of faith proved genuine.

Reads hilastērion as the mercy seat where God is reconciled, and calls justification by faith apart from works of law the grand peculiarity of the gospel, Luther's article on which the church stands or falls.
Christ offered his own body as the ransom-price of redemption, and is the true mercy seat; the righteousness of God reaches unto all and upon all who believe, Jew and Gentile without difference.

external.name: Leon Morris
external.point: Defends 'propitiation' as the proper sense of hilastērion against the reduction to mere 'expiation': the term includes the covering of sin, but Paul's argument requires that the cross also turns away God's righteous wrath, so that God can be at once just and the justifier (v.26) — mercy-seat imagery and wrath-averting both held together, not played off against each other.

21.application: Because justification is gift and not wage, “boasting is excluded” — which is at once the deepest humility and the firmest assurance, for the believer’s whole standing rests on Christ and not on self. And because the one God justifies all alike by faith (v.30), every barrier of ethnicity and status falls: the same free grace meets Jew and Gentile, the moral and the immoral, on identical terms.

_meta.title: The Righteousness of God
reflection: Paul anchors assurance not in our constancy but in God’s: “Let God be true though every one were a liar” (v.4). Where are you resting your standing before God on your own faithfulness rather than his?

The law was given not to justify but to give “the knowledge of sin” (v.20), stopping every mouth (v.19). Have you let it do its full work of silencing your defenses, so that grace can be received as gift and not wage?

At the cross God is shown to be “just and the justifier of the one who has faith in Jesus” (v.26). How does it change your worship to know God did not merely overlook your sin but bore its judgment in his Son?
